questions.prompt: DDPG stores transitions collected with earlier (outdated) policies in its replay buffer and trains on them. Why is this valid — i.e., why doesn't training on off-policy data break Q-learning?

options.text: DDPG uses importance sampling to correct for the distribution shift between old and current policy
The Bellman equation is a constraint on Q that must hold for any transition regardless of the collecting policy, so any valid transition is usable
The replay buffer only stores transitions from the last 1000 steps, so the data is approximately on-policy
Off-policy data is valid only because the deterministic policy makes the action distribution irrelevant

questions.explanation: The Bellman equation \(Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')]\) must hold for *all* valid transitions \((s,a,r,s')\) — it doesn't care which policy collected them. This is why Q-learning can use a replay buffer of old experiences: any stored transition is a valid training sample for MSBE minimization.
questions.hint: Think about what the Bellman equation is saying: it's a constraint on Q that must hold for all state-action pairs.

questions.prompt: TD3 uses `policy_delay=2` (default). What happens during the update iterations where the policy is NOT updated?

options.text: The target networks are updated but the main networks are frozen
Both Q-functions are updated but the policy and target networks are left unchanged
New environment interactions are collected to replenish the replay buffer
The exploration noise is doubled to compensate for the paused policy learning

questions.explanation: With `policy_delay=2`, for every 2 gradient update iterations, the Q-functions are updated twice but the policy (\(\mu_\theta\)) and target networks (\(\phi_{\text{targ}}, \theta_{\text{targ}}\)) are updated only once. During the 'skipped' iterations, only \(Q_{\phi_1}\) and \(Q_{\phi_2}\) are trained. This lets Q-functions stabilize before influencing the policy.
questions.hint: What specifically is being 'delayed' — the policy update or the Q-function update?

questions.prompt: SAC's entropy-regularized objective includes \(\alpha H(\pi(\cdot|s_t))\) as a bonus at each timestep. What practical effect does this have on the learned policy?

options.text: The policy converges faster because entropy regularization simplifies the optimization landscape
The policy maintains broader action distributions, improving exploration and preventing premature convergence to suboptimal behavior
The policy becomes more deterministic over time to maximize the entropy bonus
The policy can only be used in environments with continuous action spaces

questions.explanation: The entropy bonus \(\alpha H(\pi(\cdot|s))\) rewards the policy for maintaining randomness. This creates a 'soft' policy that doesn't fully commit to a single action unless the advantage of doing so clearly outweighs the entropy loss. The result: better exploration, robustness to reward noise, and resistance to premature convergence. This is also why SAC doesn't need an explicit exploration mechanism like DDPG's Gaussian noise.
questions.hint: Maximizing entropy means maximizing randomness — what does that imply about action selection?

questions.prompt: The SAC policy uses the reparameterization trick: \(\tilde{a} = \tanh(\mu_\theta(s) + \sigma_\theta(s) \odot \xi)\) where \(\xi \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)\). What does applying \(\tanh\) achieve?

options.text: It normalizes the action distribution to have zero mean and unit variance
It squashes unbounded Gaussian samples to the range \((-1, 1)\), keeping actions within valid bounds
It makes the policy deterministic by applying a monotone transformation
It converts the Gaussian distribution into a categorical distribution over discretized actions

questions.explanation: A Gaussian distribution has unbounded support — actions can be arbitrarily large. Most continuous control environments require bounded actions (e.g., joint torques in \([-1,1]\)). The \(\tanh\) squashing maps \(\mathbb{R}\) to \((-1,1)\). After rescaling to the environment's action range, all actions are valid. The resulting distribution is a squashed Gaussian — no longer Gaussian, but log-probabilities can still be computed analytically (see SAC paper appendix).
questions.hint: What is the output range of \(\tanh(x)\) for any \(x \in \mathbb{R}\)?

questions.prompt: You run PPO with 5 random seeds and want to plot the results with Spinning Up's plotter. How should you structure the output directories for the plotter to automatically average across seeds?

options.text: Save each seed to a completely separate top-level directory with a different exp_name
Use the same exp_name for all seeds — Spinning Up will place them in subdirectories under the same parent folder, which the plotter averages automatically
Manually concatenate the progress.txt files before plotting
Use --datestamp to group runs by timestamp into the same folder

questions.explanation: When you run `python -m spinup.run ppo --exp_name myexp --seed 0 10 20`, Spinning Up places each seed's output in `data/myexp/myexp_s0/`, `data/myexp/myexp_s10/`, etc. — all under the same parent `data/myexp/`. The plotter, when given `data/myexp/`, detects multiple seed subdirectories and automatically plots mean ± std.
questions.hint: Think about what happens when you pass multiple `--seed` values to spinup.run.

questions.prompt: Which metric from `progress.txt` is the most reliable indicator that a DDPG agent is actually improving?

options.text: `LossPi` — the policy gradient loss decreasing means the policy is getting better
`LossQ` — a decreasing Q-function loss means the Q-function is converging
`AverageEpRet` — the actual return in test episodes is the ground truth measure of policy quality
`QVals` — higher Q-values indicate the agent expects better performance

questions.explanation: `AverageEpRet` is the only ground-truth metric. `LossPi` in DDPG is a surrogate objective that doesn't correspond directly to policy quality. `LossQ` (MSBE) decreasing means the Q-function is fitting the Bellman equation better, which is good for training stability but doesn't directly measure policy quality. Q-values can be overestimated without the policy actually improving. Only real environment returns tell you how well the agent is doing.
questions.hint: Which metric directly measures what you actually care about — how much reward the agent collects in the real environment?
